title: Cotton
prose: CLIMATE REQUIREMENTS An average temperature of 25 °C during the entire growing season is essential. The optimum temperature is described as 34 °C during the day, and 16 °C during the night. At least 200 frost-free and cloudless days are needed to attain the high heat unit requirements. SOIL Cotton can be grown on a wide range of soils, but will perform better on deep fertile sandy loam to loam soils. Poorly drained soils should be avoided, as cotton is sensitive to waterlogging. The crop is fairly adaptable to saline conditions, but is very sensitive to low pH-conditions and aluminium toxicity. Compaction layers in the soil should be broken before planting to ensure good crop response. Soils that are inclined toward crusting can result in poor germination and low stands. The high cost of fertilizer makes it imperative that the contribution of the soil to the total nutrient requirements of the crop be taken into account. However, the farmer should guard against practices that will mine the soil of nutrients. The fertility status of the soil should, at the very least, be kept at a satisfactory level. YIELD TARGET A realistic yield target must be established in order to be able to calculate the required fertilizer levels for the crop. This yield target is influenced by factors such as: availability of irrigation water; climate; and cultivation practices. In areas that comply with the required climatic and soil conditions, realistic yield targets of, for e.g. 5 t ha -1 for seed cotton, can be achieved in the Limpopo valley. In the Loskop, Lower Orange River and the Northern Cape irrigation areas, 4 t ha -1 yields are possible. A realistic yield target for dryland conditions is between 800 and 1 200 kg ha -1 seed cotton. PLANT NUTRIENT UPTAKE Using a realistic yield target, the information in Table 5.9.1 can be consulted to indicate the quantity of each of the macro-elements removed by the crop. Table 5.9.1. Plant nutrients removed by the cotton plant above ground MACRO-ELEMENTS Nitrogen (N) Cotton reacts very positively to nitrogen applications if all production factors are optimal. A soil analysis of the available-N will only give an indication of the amount of N available at a given time. However, soil-N is not static since mineralisation of soil nitrogen is a continuous process. This process converts unavailable organic N to inorganic nitrogen that is readily available to the plant. The capability of different soils to mineralise nitrogen varies greatly. As a general rule, heavier textured soils are inclined to release more N to the plant. The quantity of N supplied through mineralisation varies from 30 to 200 kg N ha -1 , depending on soil type. A good indication for the producer, would be to note how previous crops reacted to N-fertilizer on the same soil. From these results, one would be able to deduce how much nitrogen fertilizer is mineralised on that specific soil. Available inorganic nitrogen can, however, be converted to an unavailable organic form by microbial action. This frequently occurs when large quantities of organic material with a wide carbon:nitrogen ratio (stubble from grass cereal crops) is incorporated into the soil shortly before planting. In the latter scenario, microbes responsible for the decomposition of the organic material require more nitrogen than the material can supply. Consequently, they take up the free inorganic nitrogen in the soil. The result is that the microbes effectively compete with plants for the available nitrogen in the soil. As a result, the plant does not have access to sufficient available nitrogen, thus severely hampering its growth. It is therefore clear that even though the total quantity of nitrogen being mineralised could be sufficient, a shortage of nitrogen will nevertheless occur during some stage of the growing season. The growth curve for cotton (Figure 5.9.1.) indicates clearly that more than 70% of the total nitrogen is taken up in the period 6 to 15 weeks after planting. More than 50% of all the N taken up by cotton plants is firstly translocated to the leaves before being transported to the bolls. The plant therefore requires a certain amount of time from taking up the N to actually utilising it. As a result it is recommended that all N be applied before 13 weeks after planting. It is thus clear that soil preparation and fertilization should be timed to allow for maximum utilisation to occur between 6 and 13 weeks after establishment. Any stress occurring during this period will necessarily have an influence on the final yield. Nitrogen in the nitrate form is very soluble, and therefore moves with the soil moisture. This can cause problems especially on the lighter soils. Excessive rain or irrigation can leach nitrates from the topsoil layers. It has been found that while nitrogen does not easily leach from the soil profile, it can readily leach from the top 43 cm. This can cause serious problems – particularly during the early growing period, before the plant has developed an extensive root system. Despite sufficient nitrogen content in the total soil profile, the roots can find themselves in a nitrogen deficient zone at some stage. A topdressing is therefore the best course of action, as the roots will also have access to nitrogen in the deeper layers of soil. On lighter textured soils it is therefore inadvisable to apply the total N-quantity at the outset. A much better approach is to apply the N in several applications, as long as the total application is completed by 13 weeks after planting. Together with the available information, a nitrogen balance sheet can be drawn up to determine the N-quantities to be applied. Table 5.9.2. All the information mentioned can be gained from analysis, except the mineralised-N (C). Figures in Table 5.9.3 can be used as a general guideline. Although it is a generalisation it does serve as a basis. Adaptations can be made according to historic information of the specific field. Growth regulators are generally advised if high rates of nitrogen are applied. Even though these growth regulators do not as such give a higher yield, they do control excessive vegetative growth, which makes pest control easier and limits rotting of the bolls. Growth regulators pose a serious risk on fields with very variable soils and consequently variable cotton growth. This is because of the large variance in vegetative growth on such fields. In order to apply growth regulators correctly, different growth regulator quantities are therefore required throughout, making application practically impossible. The net result of a uniform application under such circumstances is that plants on poor growth areas are completely dwarfed, while those on high vegetative growth areas are poorly regulated. The use of growth regulators is therefore largely determined by circumstances. The discretion of the farmer who is fully aware of his conditions and situation, is thus the determining factor. Table 5.9.2. Example of a Nitrogen balance sheet to a depth of 1.2 metres Table 5.9.3. Mineralisation of organically bound N to a depth of 1.2 metres Phosphorus (P) Cotton rarely reacts to P-applications. To maintain soil fertility, the recommended P-applications are reflected in Table 5.9.4. Fertilizing is thus aimed at merely replacing the phosphorus removed by the crop. It has been found that double superphosphate gives better results on soils with a high P-sorption. It is recommended that these soils (red soils) are fertilized with this carrier. Table 5.9.4. Guidelines for P-fertilization of cotton as per soil analyses Potassium (K) Potassium should be applied prior to planting. It plays an important role in respiration, protein synthesis and carbohydrate metabolism. P-deficiencies are not the sole cause of “Red death”; several other factors can cause or contribute to the phenomena. Cotton plants have a very slow reaction to K-fertilizer. Potassium applications are not recommended where soil analyses indicate levels above 80, 100 and 120 mg kg -1 for sand, loam and clay soils respectively. However, applications may be considered if the ratio is (Ca + Mg) ÷ K in cmol c kg -1 >20. On heavier textured soils, where K is normally present in high levels, or where there is a high cation reserve, this norm can be adjusted further. In such instances, the ratio can sometimes be as high as 100, but this does not necessarily indicate a K-deficiency. If, however, K is applied, the removal figures (Table 5.9.1) should not be exceeded. It has been suggested that foliar applications of potassium nitrate will improve quality rather than yield. However, no proof of this has been found in controlled trials. The practice can therefore not be recommended. MICRO-ELEMENTS There are indications that the micro-element status of cotton is already sub- optimal in certain areas. Currently, however, there is very little supportive information available. It is, therefore, recommended that producers take petiole samples a week after the appearance of the first flowers and have these analysed. This may serve to determine whether there are any deficiencies in a particular field.
